draft.introduction: The Golden Verses are moral precepts attributed to Pythagoras, though likely composed by later Pythagoreans in the 4th–3rd century BCE. Lines 1–10 establish the ethical foundations of the Pythagorean life: honor the gods, revere the heroes, and govern oneself.

translation.original_ref: Hierocles, Commentary on the Golden Verses (Mullach, FPG I.408)
translation.text: First, honor the immortal gods as custom ordains. Revere the oath. Next, honor the illustrious heroes and the spirits beneath the earth by performing the lawful rites. Honor likewise your parents and those nearest to you in blood. Of the rest, make the most virtuous your friend.
translation.original_text: ἀθανάτους μὲν πρῶτα θεούς, νόμῳ ὡς διάκεινται, τίμα· καὶ σέβου ὕρκον· ἔπειθ᾽ ἥρωας ἀγαθούς

translator_notes.note: The emphasis on honoring gods, heroes, and spirits in hierarchical order reflects Pythagorean cosmology of ordered levels of being, which later influenced Platonic and Neoplatonic emanation theories.
